turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I spent hours trying to wrap a static variable in a mutex but it feels like I'm fighting the compiler every time. The documentation is vague about when to use LazyLock versus OnceLock and makes me think I am just guessing at syntax instead of understanding the actual memory safety guarantees.

You are not just guessing; you are dealing with a fundamental difference in how Rust handles initialization order. The core distinction is that LazyLock<T> is designed specifically for lazy globals where initialization can happen at any runtime moment, while OnceLock<T> guarantees the cell has been initialized exactly once before any read occurs.

So if I want a global constant that only computes itself on first access without blocking subsequent accesses immediately, which one should I actually use? The docs say LazyLock is stable now but I can't tell the difference from the old lazy_static crate.

You should use LazyLock<T> for that specific scenario because it was built to replace lazy_static with proper thread safety via std::sync. The key is that LazyLock uses a special internal flag and atomic operations to ensure the computation happens only once, whereas OnceLock is strictly for single-writer scenarios where you might not need runtime laziness.

This distinction still feels murky. If I use LazyLock, does it automatically handle the initialization barrier so that if another thread tries to read before the computation finishes, it will just wait instead of panicking?

Exactly. When you declare a LazyLock<T>, the compiler inserts an internal synchronization mechanism that acts as a proper initialization barrier. If another thread attempts to read the value while the computation is still running, it will simply wait for the LazyLock to finish initializing rather than encountering a race condition.

Okay, so I understand the safety model now. But what about memory ordering? Does LazyLock implicitly use some specific kind of atomic operation like std::sync::atomic::Ordering that I need to worry about manually?

You do not need to specify any manual memory ordering for LazyLock. The type itself encapsulates the necessary synchronization primitives, typically using a combination of atomic flags and atomic pointers under the hood to ensure correct visibility across threads.
That simplifies things greatly. So once I have my LazyLock set up, can I just call .get() on it like a normal reference or does it require some special handling?
You simply call the get method which returns an Option<&T>. The function will return None if the value has not been initialized yet, otherwise it returns a reference to the computed value.
And what about error handling? If my lazy computation panics or returns an error, does LazyLock prevent that from propagating up?

No, LazyLock does not intercept panics. If your closure passed to the LazyLock constructor panics, it will propagate up exactly like any other panic in Rust. You must ensure your initialization logic is robust or handle errors explicitly within the closure itself.
I see. So LazyLock is basically the modern replacement for lazy_static but with better integration into std::sync, right?

Yes. It provides the same lazy initialization pattern as lazy_static but with full compatibility to Rust's standard library types and proper thread safety guarantees without requiring external crates.

Hey everyone, I've read about LazyLock and OnceLock but I still don't fully understand how to use them correctly in my project. I keep getting confused when initializing global variables because the documentation says they are initialization-safe but I'm not sure what that actually means.

The key difference is that LazyLock<T> wraps a lazy-initialized value and ensures it's computed only once, while OnceLock<T> guarantees the value is set exactly once. When using LazyLock for globals like a logger or config singleton, you can safely call it multiple times without recomputing because it caches the result internally.
That makes sense but I'm still worried about concurrency. What happens if two threads try to initialize at the same time? Does LazyLock handle that automatically or do I need extra synchronization?

LazyLock handles all of that internally using atomic operations and memory barriers, so you don't need to add any manual locking. It's safe for multi-threaded use without explicit locks because it uses std::sync::atomic primitives under the hood.
Okay, I think I understand LazyLock now but what about OnceLock? Can I replace lazy_static with either of these and are there any gotchas I should watch out for?

You can absolutely replace lazy_static with LazyLock or OnceCell depending on whether you need lazy initialization. The main difference is that OnceLock doesn't support lazy evaluation - it only works when the value is computed before being stored, whereas LazyLock delays computation until first access.

I'm debugging a memory leak in our production service where we're initializing expensive configuration objects on the first request. We currently use lazy_static for singletons, but I need something thread-safe that guarantees initialization happens only once per thread without blocking other requests.

The memory leak likely stems from holding references to unowned data across threads or using a global static that gets re-initialized on every spawn. For true singletons, std::sync::LazyLock is the correct choice as it wraps your global state and ensures safe initialization without panics if already initialized. If you only need a write-once cell per thread, OnceLock is better for local scope because LazyLock requires a static variable.
Right, so I can't use LazyLock inside a function? What happens if the initialization fails with LazyLock compared to OnceLock?

LazyLock panics on initialization failure at runtime which is acceptable for globals since they must always be initialized, while OnceLock returns an Option<T> so it can safely skip initialization if the closure fails. You should wrap your expensive config builder in a LazyLock static variable or use OnceLock with a guard condition to prevent panics during graceful shutdowns.
